channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - techraptor.net
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for techraptor.net

item.title: Resident Evil Requiem Sales Have Now Passed 7m
item.content:encoded:
Resident Evil Requiem sales have passed yet another major milestone, reinforcing the game's status as the fastest-selling in its franchise and probably making everyone who worked on it very proud indeed.

As acknowledged by director Koshi Nakanishi on Instagram (thanks, Twisted Voxel), Resident Evil Requiem has now passed the seven million sales mark. That means it's landed another million sales since last month, and given its status as an exclusively single-player experience, that's reason to be excited.

Of course, Resident Evil Requiem was a success right out of the gate. Less than a week after its initial launch, the game passed five million sales, and it broke Steam records for the franchise on release as well, hitting over 300,000 players in just a few hours.

Resident Evil Requiem is the ninth mainline entry in the long-running Resident Evil series (note the sneaky Q doubling up as a 9 in the title). It revolves around two protagonists: Grace Ashcroft, the daughter of Outbreak character Alyssa Ashcroft, and returning hero Leon S. Kennedy.

Grace's sections take on a much more sedate, traditional survival horror pace, while Leon's are more action-oriented; after all, following the insanity of Resident Evil 4 and 6, it's not really possible to sell Leon as a scared horror protagonist anymore.

Following the successful launch of Resident Evil Requiem, Nakanishi confirmed that a story expansion is in the works, and that the game will continue to be updated with new content and features, so if you felt like you were done with Requiem, you may want to rethink that stance.

Resident Evil Requiem is available right now for PC, PlayStation 5, Xbox Series X|S, and Nintendo Switch 2, and you can read our very own Andrew Stretch's review of the game right here if you want to know whether it's worth your time or not.

Joe has been writing for TechRaptor for several years, and in those years has learned a lot about the gaming industry and its foibles. He’s originally an… More about Joseph

item.title: Disgaea Mayhem Release Date Brings the Chaos in July
item.content:encoded:
NIS America has announced that Disgaea Mayhem, the latest spinoff of the popular tactical RPG series, is coming to the West in July, and it's losing none of the series' signature over-the-top fun, either.

First revealed in February, Disgaea Mayhem is landing on PC and consoles on July 23rd, and that includes the Switch 2, although you'll also be able to play it on the original Switch if you prefer.

The game represents a "brand new take on the Disgaea franchise", according to NIS America; it swaps the series' thoughtful (if wacky) tactical battling for a more direct, hands-on action RPG approach, so expect to get stuck into combat rather than directing things from the skies as usual.

You'll play as the mercenary N.A., who must help Princess Tichelle to acquire as much dessert as she possibly can in order to score a massive paycheck. It is, as that summary suggests, a "light-hearted tale", in NIS America's words.

This being Disgaea, you can also look forward to seven different weapons to wield, each of which offers a different playstyle for you to master. Naturally, you can switch between them whenever you like as well, so you're not limited to just one combat style.

Also in keeping for Disgaea are the absurd damage numbers; NIS America says you'll be "pass[ing] new bills at the Dark Chocolate Assembly to make the numbers go up until you're dealing a million damage with every hit".

If you're a fan of the Warriors series and its various spinoffs, you'll probably enjoy this one, but you'll love it even more if you're also a Disgaea aficionado. You can check out the new release date trailer right here.

Disgaea Mayhem is releasing for PC, PlayStation 5, and both Switch 2 and Switch on July 23rd. Make sure to wishlist this one if you're looking for some larger-than-life RPG action.

Joe has been writing for TechRaptor for several years, and in those years has learned a lot about the gaming industry and its foibles. He’s originally an… More about Joseph
